section_name: CSRF: AJAX dispatch without nonce verification
description: AJAX handler registered — files matching this but lacking nonce verification may have CSRF vulnerabilities

section_name: HTTP Header Injection via dynamic Content-Disposition
description: HTTP header() with Content-Disposition/Type — if filename/type is user-controlled without sanitization, CRLF injection possible

section_name: Path Traversal: file ops on unsanitized paths
description: File read/include with no realpath() or prefix check — if path comes from user input or stored settings, path traversal possible

section_name: Broken Access Control: admin_action without capability check
description: admin-post/admin-action handler without capability check — nonce alone is not authorization

section_name: Unauth handler: wp_ajax_nopriv with state mutation
description: Unauthenticated AJAX handler — any state-changing operation here is exposed to anonymous users. Check what it does.

section_name: CSS/Style Injection via unescaped style attributes
description: Dynamic CSS injection via inline styles or wp_add_inline_style with user-controlled values
Redirect with user-controlled URL — without esc_url/wp_validate_redirect, open redirect or parameter injection possible

section_name: Sensitive Data Exposure: order/user data in exports
description: WooCommerce order/billing/shipping data access — ensure proper capability gating to prevent unauthorized data export
Direct echo of apply_filters() return without escaping — any plugin hooking the filter can inject HTML/script

section_name: Missing Content-Type: JSON via echo json_encode
description: AJAX handler returns JSON via echo json_encode() instead of wp_send_json() — response served as text/html enables MIME-sniffing XSS

section_name: SQL Injection: implode() array into IN() clause
description: Array imploded directly into SQL IN() clause without integer cast — if array comes from filter/user input, SQL injection possible

Matches any call to unserialize() that does not specify the allowed_classes option. PHP unserialize without allowed_classes can trigger __wakeup/__destruct on arbitrary classes present in the codebase (POP chain), making it exploitable whenever the serialized input originates from DB rows, options, or transients written by users or other plugins.

section_name: SQL Injection: unguarded concatenation into SQL WHERE clause via implode
description: Catches direct string concatenation into a SQL where clause using implode() on an array, without integer sanitization. Common pattern in WooCommerce filter integration code where post ID arrays flow through WordPress hooks and are then embedded in raw SQL.

section_name: Missing Input Sanitization: Raw $_POST nested key passed to search
description: Detects places where a nested $_POST value used as a search phrase is not sanitized before use. Without upstream sanitization, security relies entirely on downstream callers — removing or changing those callers introduces injection risk.
